era_context: The year 762 CE falls during the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE) in China, the consolidation of the Abbasid Caliphate in the Islamic world, and the reign of Pepin III in the Frankish Kingdom. This was a period of significant military conflict and administrative reorganization across major Eurasian powers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish realm

events.title: Foundation of Baghdad by Caliph al-Mansur
events.description: Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) founded Baghdad as the new capital of the Abbasid Caliphate in 762 CE. The city was designed as a circular, fortified settlement with the caliph's palace at the center, representing a revolutionary model of Islamic urbanism and centralized governance. The site was chosen for its strategic location on the Tigris River, at the intersection of trade routes connecting the Mediterranean, Central Asia, and the Indian Ocean. Construction continued until 766 CE, and Baghdad quickly became one of the largest and most cosmopolitan cities in the world. The founding of Baghdad symbolized the Abbasid shift from Arab tribal governance to a centralized, multi-ethnic, Persian-influenced bureaucratic state.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)
al-Khatib al-Baghdadi, History of Baghdad (Tarikh Baghdad)

events.certainty_note: The founding of Baghdad in 762 CE is clearly documented in Islamic historical sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence. The date is well-attested.

events.title: Tang Dynasty: Emperor Daizong's accession; continuation of An Lushan Rebellion recovery
events.description: Emperor Daizong of Tang (r. 762–779 CE) ascended to the throne in 762 CE following the death of his father, Emperor Suzong. Daizong inherited a dynasty weakened by the ongoing An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE), which was still in its final stages. His reign was marked by efforts to restore central authority, deal with the rise of regional military governors (jiedushi), and manage fiscal crisis caused by the rebellion. Despite these challenges, Daizong's reign saw continued cultural production and the development of new administrative structures.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu), compiled by Ouyang Xiu et al., 11th century
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu), compiled by Liu Xu et al., 10th century

events.certainty_note: The accession of Emperor Daizong in 762 CE is clearly documented in Chinese historical sources. The date is well-attested.

events.title: Frankish Kingdom: Pepin III continues military consolidation
events.region: Frankish Kingdom (modern France, Germany, Low Countries)
events.description: Pepin III (r. 751–768 CE) continued his military campaigns in the early 760s, consolidating Frankish power in Aquitaine and the Pyrenees. By 762, Pepin had established the Carolingian dynasty as the dominant military power in Western Europe. His campaigns against Aquitanian nobles and preparations for the succession of his sons, Charlemagne and Carloman, were ongoing. These military campaigns and administrative consolidations provided the foundation for Charlemagne's later expansion and the creation of the Carolingian Empire.
events.certainty_note: The Royal Frankish Annals provide near-contemporary documentation of Frankish military campaigns in the 760s, though specific events in 762 CE are not always detailed.

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion: Continued fighting in northern China
events.description: The An Lushan Rebellion, which had begun in 755 CE, continued in its seventh year in 762 CE. By this time, the initial rebel leader An Lushan had died (in 757 CE), and his successor Shi Siming was leading the rebellion. The conflict had devastated northern China, disrupted trade routes, and severely weakened the Tang Dynasty's military and fiscal capacity. Fighting continued in the northern regions, with Tang forces gradually gaining ground. The rebellion would finally be suppressed in 763 CE.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu)

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the An Lushan Rebellion in 762 CE is clearly documented in Chinese historical sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The founding of Baghdad is often presented as an immediate transformation of Islamic governance and culture. However, the transition from Umayyad to Abbasid administrative models was gradual, and Baghdad's completion in 766 CE did not immediately resolve factional tensions within the caliphate or establish it as the undisputed center of Islamic civilization. Additionally, the significance of Baghdad's founding can obscure the continued importance of other Islamic cities like Damascus and the ongoing challenges of consolidating Abbasid power across the vast empire.
historiographic_note: Sources for 762 CE are concentrated in the Islamic world and China, where court historiography was well-developed. Islamic sources for Baghdad's founding are often compiled centuries after the event (al-Tabari wrote in the 9th century, al-Khatib al-Baghdadi in the 11th century) and reflect later theological and political perspectives. Chinese sources are more contemporary but still reflect court perspectives. Western European sources are sparse and often legendary. The year 762 CE is significant primarily for events in the Islamic world and China; most of the rest of the world has no documented events for this year.

graph_edges.note: Baghdad's founding was part of the Abbasid consolidation following the revolution of 750 CE
Daizong's accession occurred during the An Lushan Rebellion
Continued fighting in 762 CE led to the rebellion's suppression in 763 CE
Pepin III's consolidation prepared the way for Charlemagne's succession
